Before judgment is pronounced, however, it must be observed whether prosperities overcome misfortunes, or the reverse. For if they are equal, the judgment is very difficult: therefore, they must be diligently considered.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Acute or Chronic Illness
text: CAP. X. POSTEA QUAM an curabilis sit morbus necne, monstratum est. Si curabilem esse agnoscamus: reliquum est ut an acutus sit vel chronicus, id est, an brevis vel longus, videamus. Quod ut agnoscamus sexta domus, eius dominus, Luna, dominus ascendentis, planeta à quo separatur, et ea cui applicat animadvertendi sunt. Si enim sextae domus signum ex fixorum numero sit, et eius dominus sit in signo fixo situs, vel planeta gravis (ut sunt Saturnus et Iupiter) fuerit, morbi longitudinem praenuntiant. Si autem in mobilibus signis collocatus fuerit, et in sexta mobile signum fuerit, morbi brevitatem facit. Si denique fixorum et mobilium loco, communia sive media adsunt, morbi residuam timere iubent.
translation: CHAPTER X. AFTER it has been shown whether the disease is curable or not. If we recognize it to be curable, it remains for us to see whether it is acute or chronic, that is, whether short or long. To ascertain this, the sixth house, its lord, the Moon, the lord of the ascendant, the planet from which it separates, and the planet to which it applies, must be observed. For if the sign of the sixth house is among the fixed signs, and its lord is situated in a fixed sign, or if it is a heavy planet (such as Saturn and Jupiter), they portend a long duration of the illness. But if it is placed in mutable signs, and there is a mutable sign in the sixth, it makes the illness short. If, finally, instead of fixed and mutable, common or medium signs are present, they warn to fear a lingering illness.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon's Motion and Illness Duration
text: Simili via et ratione de planeta à quo separatur dominus ascendentis iudicandum est. Luna etiam animadvertenda est. Si enim in suo motu tardior sit, et lumine crescat, morbi augmentum denuntiat: ut etiam si in morbi principio et in sui incremento cum Marte et in decremento cum Saturno sit: morbi perniciem eiusque longitudinem praesagit. Idem etiam praestat si in signo fixo et infortunata, id est malè collocata sit. Quando item in morbi principio Luna à combustione exit, infirmitas crescit, quousque Luna ad loci in quo erat, in morbi insultu oppositionem veniat, quo in loco si fortunatos planetas inveniat, morbus iudicabitur. Si verò infortunatos aut morietur aeger aut diuturnior morbus erit. Sed de his latius in sequentibus, cum de crisibus fiet sermo, dicemus.
translation: In a similar way and by the same reasoning, judgment must be made concerning the planet from which the lord of the ascendant separates. The Moon must also be observed. For if it is slower in its motion and increases in light, it portends an increase in the illness: as also if at the beginning of the illness and at its increase it is with Mars, and at its decrease with Saturn, it presages the destruction of the illness and its long duration. The same also happens if it is in a fixed sign and unfortunate, that is, badly placed. When the Moon also emerges from combustion at the beginning of the illness, the infirmity increases until the Moon reaches the opposition of the place where it was at the onset of the illness; in which place, if it finds fortunate planets, the illness will be judged [to improve]. But if unfortunate ones, the patient will either die or the illness will be more prolonged. But we will speak more broadly about these things later, when we discuss crises.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Illness Duration Calculation
text: Dominus item domus Lunae animadvertendus est, qui si infortunatus sit, morbi longitudinem arguit. Morbi etiam longitudo ut Hali inquit, à domini ascendentis cum fortunatis vel infortunatis vicinitate sumitur. Si enim ascendentis dominus ad planetam fortunatum subito coniunctione vel aspectu pervenit, brevem morbum facit: si autem tarde, longum. Partes item fortunae, vitae, mortis et planetae interficientis (quibus omnibus fortunae pars antecellit) animadvertendae sunt. Quando itaque partes illae benè collocatae sunt, et in signis mobilibus, morbi brevitatem: si autem malè et in signis fixis, longitudinem praenuntiat. Tempus autem longitudinis morbi invenitur, si gradus qui sunt inter applicationem significatorum cum fortunatis numerantur, et pro gradu quolibet dies una sumatur, si signum mobile sit: si verò sit fixum, hebdomadam vel mensem vel annum: si autem commune sit, diem vel hebdomadam vel mensem dabimus: quia cum significatores fortunatis applicant, tunc morbus iudicatur.
translation: The lord of the Moon's house must also be observed; if he is unfortunate, it indicates a long duration of the illness. The length of the illness, as Hali says, is also taken from the proximity of the lord of the ascendant to benefics or malefics. For if the lord of the ascendant reaches a fortunate planet by sudden conjunction or aspect, it makes the illness short; but if slowly, long. The Parts of Fortune, Life, Death, and the Killing Planet (among which the Part of Fortune excels all others) must also be observed. Therefore, when these parts are well-placed and in mutable signs, they portend a short illness; but if badly placed and in fixed signs, a long one. The time of the illness's duration is found if the degrees between the application of the significators with the benefics are counted, and for each degree one day is taken if the sign is mutable; but if it is fixed, we will give a week or a month or a year; if it is common, a day or a week or a month: because when the significators apply to benefics, then the illness is judged.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Definition and Types of Crisis
text: CAP. XI. SUPEREST nunc ut de criticis diebus ac eorum indicativis et intercidentibus quidpiam dicamus. Quae res ut clarius intelligatur, quid sit crisis initio dicendum est. Omnis enim quae à ratione de aliqua re institutio suscipitur, à definitione debet proficisci, ut intelligatur quid sit de quo disputatur. Crisin igitur Galenus in commentario 13. sectione secunda aphorismorum et commentario 230. et libro primo epidemion commentario 3. et tertio prognosticorum commentario primo, dicit esse vehementem in morbis mutationem ad salutem vel mortem. Non me latet dictionem iudicium à medicis multis sumi modis. Sed nunc in ea significatione à Galeno in iam dictis locis accepta, capitur: nempe pro subita mutatione in morbo ad salutem vel ad mortem. Crisis autem alia est perfecta, alia imperfecta. Perfecta est quando morbus integre et absolute iudicatur. Eaque duplex, perfecta laudabilis, cum scilicet, morbus ad salutem iudicatur: et perfecta illaudabilis, cum morbus integre iudicatur, sed ad mortem. Imperfecta est quando integre morbus non iudicatur: sed ex eo quidpiam relinquitur, et residuam facit: idque dupliciter. Vel enim morbus paulatim ad mortem tendit, et mala iudicatio dicitur: vel paulatim ad salutem accedit, et laudabilis imperfecta vocatur.
translation: CHAPTER XI. It remains now for us to say something about critical days and their indicative and intercident days. So that this matter may be more clearly understood, it must first be stated what a crisis is. For every institution that is undertaken by reason concerning some matter ought to proceed from a definition, so that it may be understood what is being discussed. Galen, therefore, in commentary 13, second section of the Aphorisms, and commentary 230, and in the first book of Epidemics, commentary 3, and the third of Prognostics, first commentary, says that a crisis is a vehement change in diseases towards health or death. It does not escape me that the word 'judgment' is taken by many physicians in various ways. But now it is taken in that sense accepted by Galen in the places already mentioned: namely, for a sudden change in the disease towards health or death. A crisis, moreover, is either perfect or imperfect. Perfect is when the disease is judged completely and absolutely. And this is twofold: perfect laudable, when, namely, the disease is judged towards health; and perfect unlaudable, when the disease is judged completely, but towards death. Imperfect is when the disease is not judged completely: but something remains from it, and it causes a residue; and this in two ways. For the disease gradually tends towards death, and is called a bad judgment; or it gradually approaches health, and is called a laudable imperfect crisis.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Ptolemy's Critical Day Method
text: Crisium item alia est salva, quae sine magnis et perniciosis accidentibus sit. Alia dubia, quae scilicet, cum illis advenit. Alia denique est iudicata, ut quando die quarta signa coctionis apparent, morbum septimo die iudicaturum denuntiant. Non iudicata alia, ut illa quae septimo die advenit, non tamen die quarta praesignificata fuit. Alia insuper in critico die sit, alia autem minimè. Qui verò critici dies sint in sequentibus dicetur. Medici Hippocratem et Galenum sequentes, alio modo quam astrologi criticos dies accipiunt. Galenus tamen tertio libro de decretoriis diebus eos accipiendos esse à motu Lunae iudicat. Quoniam, ut capite quinto eiusdem libri inquit, mutationes aegritudinum à Luna proveniunt. Ut si quis in morbum inciderit, Luna in Tauro existente, mutationem dum in Leone erit, vel Scorpione, vel Aquario sentiet. Haec enim signa quadrato aspectu et oppositione Taurum aspiciunt. Sed quoniam Galenus viam Ptolemaei et rationem adhuc non assequitur: Nos rationem tradere cupientes rem ipsam aggredimur. Ptolemaeus aphorismo sexagesimo centiloquii inquit. Super aegrotis criticos inspice, ac Luna peragrationem in angulis figurae sexdecim laterum: ubi enim eos benè affectos inveneris, benè erit languenti: contra verò male ubi affectos inveneris. Si igitur secundum hanc doctrinam circulus in quatuor quadrantes dividatur: et quod signum et quaenam signi pars quadrantis finem attingat, videatur: dies in qua crisis futura est facilè dignoscetur.
translation: Of crises, some are safe, which occur without great and pernicious accidents. Others are doubtful, which, namely, come with such accidents. Still others are judged, as when on the fourth day signs of concoction appear, they announce that the disease will be judged on the seventh day. Others are unjudged, as those that arrive on the seventh day, but were not pre-signified on the fourth day. Moreover, some occur on a critical day, others not at all. Which days are critical will be said in the following. Physicians following Hippocrates and Galen understand critical days differently than astrologers. Galen, however, in the third book on decretory days, judges that they are to be taken from the Moon's motion. For, as he says in the fifth chapter of the same book, changes in illnesses arise from the Moon. For example, if someone falls ill when the Moon is in Taurus, they will feel a change when it is in Leo, or Scorpio, or Aquarius. For these signs aspect Taurus with a square aspect and opposition. But since Galen does not yet follow Ptolemy's path and reasoning, we, wishing to provide the reasoning, approach the matter itself. Ptolemy says in the sixtieth aphorism of the Centiloquium: 'Observe the critical days for the sick, and the Moon's peregrination in the angles of a sixteen-sided figure: for where you find them well-affected, it will be well for the sick; but conversely, badly where you find them ill-affected.' If, therefore, according to this doctrine, the circle is divided into four quadrants: and it is seen what sign and what part of the sign reaches the end of the quadrant, the day on which the crisis will occur will be easily recognized.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon's Condition and Crisis Timing
text: Cum enim Luna ad hunc angulum (angulum, scilicet eum qui quarto aspectu locum in quo Luna in morbi initio erat, aspicit) pervenerit: et tunc benè affecta fuerit, id est cum benevolis planetis aspectum habuerit, vel stellis primae vel secundae magnitudinis naturae Iovis vel Veneris iuncta fuerit, eo tempore crisis ad salutem fiet. Si autem malè affecta fuerit, quadrato vel oppositione Saturni vel Martis, aut stellis primae et secundae magnitudinis naturae eorum iuncta sit: tunc crisis non fiet, aut si fiat, ad damnum potius quam ad salutem erit: et praecipuè si calidus sit morbus, et Martem Luna aspexerit, qui caliditate sua morbum fovet. Luna autem aliquando celerius, aliquando tardius hunc angulum pervenit, quo fit, ut crisis aliquando sexquiquinto die, quando scilicet, Luna celeriter movetur: aliquando etiam sexto crisis apparere incipiat. Cum verò medio motu Luna movetur, sesquisexto die vel propè septimi diei finem morbus iudicatur. Cum aut tardius Luna movetur, crises vel octavo die vel tardius fiunt. Et quoniam in omnibus modus tenendus est, ideò crises septimo die perfectiores, quam aliis diebus fiunt. Crisis etiam laudabilis aliquando fit die quinto: tunc enim Luna propter motus velocitatem fortunata est. Quod si contingat crisim non fieri Luna in hoc primo existente. Animadvertendum est quo tempore ad oppositum loci in quo erat, tempore inceptionis morbi perveniet (Ad eum autem ut plurimum die decimoquarto pervenit.) Si enim (ut de primo angulo dictum est) bene affecta Luna fuerit: ad salutem crisis fiet. Si autem malè affecta fuerit: ad perniciem potius quam salutem fiet, aut non fiet.
translation: For when the Moon reaches this angle (namely, the angle which aspects with a square aspect the place where the Moon was at the beginning of the illness): and then is well-affected, that is, has an aspect with benevolent planets, or is joined to stars of the first or second magnitude of the nature of Jupiter or Venus, at that time the crisis will be towards health. But if it is ill-affected, by a square or opposition of Saturn or Mars, or joined to stars of the first and second magnitude of their nature: then the crisis will not occur, or if it does, it will be to harm rather than to health; and especially if the disease is hot, and the Moon aspects Mars, who fosters the disease with his heat. The Moon, however, sometimes reaches this angle more quickly, sometimes more slowly, which means that the crisis sometimes begins to appear on the fifth day and a half, when, namely, the Moon moves quickly; sometimes even on the sixth. But when the Moon moves with a medium motion, the disease is judged on the sixth and a half day or near the end of the seventh day. But when the Moon moves more slowly, crises occur on the eighth day or later. And since moderation must be observed in all things, therefore crises on the seventh day are more perfect than on other days. A laudable crisis also sometimes occurs on the fifth day: for then the Moon is fortunate due to the speed of its motion. If it happens that a crisis does not occur with the Moon in this first position, it must be observed at what time it will reach the opposite of the place where it was at the time of the illness's inception (it usually reaches it on the fourteenth day). For if (as was said of the first angle) the Moon is well-affected: the crisis will be towards health. But if it is ill-affected: it will be towards harm rather than health, or it will not occur.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Astrological Rationale for Critical Days
text: Id verò eo tempore advenit: quoniam locus initii morbi et locus ei oppositus qualitatibus lumine et aspectu pugnat, et contrarii sunt. Et quoniam signa quadrato aspectu sese aspicientia aliquando totis qualitatibus pugnant: quando Luna signum in quo erat in morbi insultu quadrato aspectu respicit, natura adversus morbum insurgit, et nititur expellere et evacuare quod sibi noxium est. Quare si eo tempore inferior radix una cum superiore radice occurrat: tunc optimum sit iudicium. Signa autem quadrato aspectu sese aspicientia aliquando una tantum qualitate pugnant: atque tunc in quadratis radix superior debilior est. Tertius angulus (si in secundo iudicium non fiat) considerandus venit. Quoniam Luna in hoc angulo existente, id quod ut plurimum vigesima die fit, si causae supradictae occurrant iudicium apparere incipiet. Quod si non appareat, quartus angulus, qui idem est cum morbi initio, respiciendus est: quia tunc ad locum in quo erat in morbi principio, Luna pervenit. Id aut vigesimoseptimo die et tertia unius diei parte fiet: quo in loco Luna existente, superioribus et inferioribus causis occurrentibus iudicium fiet: quo loco si non fiat, morbus in acutorum numero annumerandus non est: sed in chronicorum numerum ponendus est. Ideò iam ad Solis motum redire convenit. Sol enim morbos chronicos, ut Luna acutos suo motu moderatur. Videndum est igitur in quo signo Sol in morbi principio erat: quoniam ut de Luna diximus, quando ad primum quadratum huius loci vel oppositionem, aut secundum quadratum, aut ad locum initii morbi pervenerit: morbus iudicabitur, modo ea.
translation: This happens at that time because the place of the illness's inception and the place opposite to it contend in qualities, light, and aspect, and are contrary. And because signs aspecting each other with a square aspect sometimes contend with all their qualities: when the Moon aspects with a square aspect the sign in which it was at the onset of the illness, nature rises up against the disease and strives to expel and evacuate what is harmful to it. Therefore, if at that time the inferior root occurs together with the superior root, then the judgment is excellent. However, signs aspecting each other with a square aspect sometimes contend with only one quality: and then in squares the superior root is weaker. The third angle (if judgment is not made in the second) comes to be considered. For when the Moon is in this angle, which usually happens on the twentieth day, if the aforementioned causes occur, judgment will begin to appear. But if it does not appear, the fourth angle, which is the same as the beginning of the illness, must be looked at: because then the Moon reaches the place where it was at the beginning of the illness. This will happen on the twenty-seventh day and a third part of one day: in which place, with the Moon existing and the superior and inferior causes occurring, judgment will be made: if judgment is not made in this place, the disease is not to be counted among acute ones, but is to be placed in the number of chronic ones. Therefore, it is now appropriate to return to the Sun's motion. For the Sun moderates chronic diseases by its motion, just as the Moon moderates acute ones. It must therefore be seen in what sign the Sun was at the beginning of the illness: for, as we said of the Moon, when it reaches the first square of this place or the opposition, or the second square, or the place of the illness's inception, the disease will be judged, provided those things are present.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Critical, Indicative, Intercident Days
text: Circulus ergo in quatuor aequales partes divisus, quatuor dies sive angulos criticos ostendit. Hi autem critici suos indicativos habent, qui si iterum quaelibet quarta circuli pars in duas aequales partes dividatur, invenientur: et ita totus circulus in partes aequales octo divisus erit, quarum una quaelibet gradus quinque et quadraginta comprehendit. Totus enim Zodiacus ut omnes alii circuli sphaerae gradus 360 continet, qui si in quatuor partes secantur, pars quaelibet gradus nonaginta amplectetur. Si iterum una quaelibet quarta in partes duas aequales dividatur, gradus quadraginta quinque una continebit. Luna igitur in huiuscemodi angulo animadvertenda est: cum enim ad angulum illum, qui est inter morbi initium et primum angulum criticum (secundum signorum ordinem) pervenerit, quid factura sit natura die critico, illo die monstrabit. Id autem ut plurimum quarto die fit: quia inter septimum et morbi initium medius est. In illo autem die si signa coctionis appareant, futuri iudicii cum Luna ad criticum angulum pervenerit, signum est: modò tamen causae iam dictae adsint. Si verò in primo angulo critico iudicium non fiat: cum ad medium locum primi et secundi anguli Luna pervenerit, id quod die undecimo saepe fit, an crisis decimoquarto die futura sit necne, natura ostendet. Quod si eo loco crisis non fiat, in aliis angulis indicativis et criticis ut dictum iam fuit, Luna animadvertenda est. Angulus enim qui est inter secundum angulum criticum et tertium, tertii anguli critici, indicativus angulus ad quem saepius decimo septimo die Luna pervenit. Angulus autem inter tertium angulum criticum et quartum, quarti anguli critici indicativus est: ad quem etiam vigesimocuarto die motu proprio Luna accedit. Ad haec notandum est diem certum, quo Luna ad angulos pervenit, non posse assignari. secundum enim ipsius motus celeritatem vel tarditatem aliquando citius: aliquando verò tardius eos attingit. Sed qui arithmetices non omnino ignari sunt, supputatione et calculo diem et horam, qua ad eos pervenit, facilè agnoscet. Et ideò diem et horam iudicii facilè praedicere poterit. Accidentia autem eo die futura ex fortunis vel infortuniis Lunae iudicabit. Hi dies et anguli intercidentes suos proprios habent: qui si unaquaelibet ipsarum octo partium circuli in duas aequales partes dividatur, apparebunt. Ita enim iuxta Ptolemaei doctrinam, circulus totus in partes aequales sexdecim, sive angulos divisus erit, et una quaelibet pars gradus duos et viginti cum minutis triginta continebit. Primus autem angulus, inter initium morbi et primum indicativum intercidens erit. Secundus angulus primae crisis indicativus est. Tertius verò angulus, inter indicativum et primam crisim intercidens erit. Quartus autem angulus, qui et quadratus dicitur, prima crisis est. Ita igitur inter morbi initium et primam crisim sive criticum angulum, intercidentes duo et indicativus unus erunt. Tot itaque in unaquaque reliquarum trium quartarum anguli sunt annumerandi. Quatuor ergo sunt critici dies, et totidem indicativi, ac intercidentes octo, in quibus Luna animadvertenda venit: quae etiam singulis diebus non minori negotio animadvertenda est.
translation: The circle, therefore, divided into four equal parts, shows four critical days or angles. These critical days have their indicative days, which will be found if each fourth part of the circle is again divided into two equal parts: and thus the whole circle will be divided into eight equal parts, each of which comprehends forty-five degrees. For the entire Zodiac, like all other circles of the sphere, contains 360 degrees, which if divided into four parts, each part will embrace ninety degrees. If again each fourth part is divided into two equal parts, each will contain forty-five degrees. The Moon, therefore, must be observed in such an angle: for when it reaches that angle, which is between the beginning of the illness and the first critical angle (according to the order of the signs), what nature will do on the critical day, it will show on that day. This usually happens on the fourth day: because it is midway between the seventh and the beginning of the illness. On that day, if signs of concoction appear, it is a sign of future judgment when the Moon reaches the critical angle, provided, however, that the already mentioned causes are present. But if judgment is not made in the first critical angle: when the Moon reaches the midpoint between the first and second angles, which often happens on the eleventh day, nature will show whether a crisis will occur on the fourteenth day or not. If a crisis does not occur in that place, the Moon must be observed in the other indicative and critical angles, as has already been said. For the angle which is between the second critical angle and the third, is the indicative angle of the third critical angle, to which the Moon most often arrives on the seventeenth day. The angle between the third critical angle and the fourth is the indicative angle of the fourth critical angle, to which the Moon also arrives by its own motion on the twenty-fourth day. To these points, it must be noted that a certain day on which the Moon reaches the angles cannot be assigned. For according to the speed or slowness of its motion, it sometimes reaches them sooner, sometimes later. But those who are not entirely ignorant of arithmetic will easily recognize the day and hour when it arrives at them by calculation and reckoning. And therefore, they will easily be able to predict the day and hour of judgment. The events that will occur on that day will be judged from the Moon's fortunes or misfortunes. These intercident days and angles have their own specific ones: which will appear if each of those eight parts of the circle is divided into two equal parts. Thus, according to Ptolemy's doctrine, the entire circle will be divided into sixteen equal parts or angles, and each part will contain two degrees and twenty-two and a half minutes. The first angle, moreover, will be intercident between the beginning of the illness and the first indicative. The second angle is the indicative of the first crisis. The third angle, however, will be intercident between the indicative and the first crisis. The fourth angle, which is also called the square, is the first crisis. Thus, between the beginning of the illness and the first crisis or critical angle, there will be two intercident and one indicative angle. So many angles are to be counted in each of the remaining three quarters. Therefore, there are four critical days, and as many indicative, and eight intercident days, in which the Moon must be observed: which must also be observed with no less diligence on individual days.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Practical Use of Critical Days
text: Eo enim die quo benè affecta fuerit, benè agetur cum aegro: sed praecipuè in huiuscemodi angulis. Haec est summa divisionis crisium, quare de his dicta sufficiant. Nos in studiosorum gratiam tabulam in fine tertii capitis primi libri apposuimus, dies criticos indicantes et intercidentes complectentem, cum indice planetarum dignitates et ipsos dies demonstrante. Cum quis ergo die, quo alicuius morbi iudicium sive crisis fiet, notum facere cupit, primò quodnam signum et quae signi pars Luna in morbi principio tenebat, inquirat: tum deinde super illud signum, et illam signi partem morbi initii indicem in signorum circulo apponat: tum demum indice criticorum dierum supra intercidentes indicativos et criticos angulos apposito: signa et signorum partes illos constituentes in directo lineae fiduciae apparebunt: quando enim ad haec signa et has signi partes Luna pervenit, intercidentes iudicati et critici dies sole clarius elucescunt. An fortunata etiam vel infortunata Luna sit ex situ illius, in dignitatum planetarum tabula his ea de causa supposita, apparet. Toto igitur morbi decursu ad hanc tabulam recurrendum est, ut quo die gravior vel levior sit morbus, facilè dignosci possit.
translation: For on that day when it is well-affected, the patient will fare well; but especially in such angles. This is the sum of the division of crises, therefore let what has been said about them suffice. For the benefit of students, we have appended a table at the end of the third chapter of the first book, embracing critical and intercident days, with an index demonstrating the dignities of the planets and the days themselves. Therefore, when someone wishes to know the day on which the judgment or crisis of any disease will occur, let him first inquire what sign and what part of the sign the Moon occupied at the beginning of the illness: then let him place the index of the illness's beginning upon that sign and that part of the sign in the circle of signs: then finally, with the index of critical days placed above the intercident, indicative, and critical angles: the signs and parts of signs constituting them will appear in the direct line of confidence: for when the Moon reaches these signs and these parts of signs, the intercident, judged, and critical days shine forth more clearly than the sun. Whether the Moon is fortunate or unfortunate also appears from its position, in the table of planetary dignities appended for this reason. Therefore, throughout the entire course of the illness, this table must be consulted, so that it may be easily recognized on which day the illness is more severe or lighter.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Treatment Principles and Moon's Influence
text: CAP. XII. QUA RATIONE morbi, eorum causae et partes laborantes dignosci queant, in praecedentibus dictum fuit. An verò sanabiles aut insanabiles, acuti aut chronici, et quo tempore iudicari debeant, etiam anteà monstratum est. Nunc autem qua ratione curandi sunt, et quo tempore remedia sunt adhibenda ostendere convenit. Igitur ut rem paucis ostendam, notandum est corpora inferiora à superioribus regi, et praecipuè ipsius Lunae motu morbi variantur: non quod viribus et potentia reliquis praestet: sed quoniam propinquior et motu celerior est. Videmus enim crescere. Ossa in plenilunio medulla repleta: omnia tam in mari quam in terra humore aucta Luna humores crescere: veluti cum decrescit, defunt. Cancri, astaci, et ostreae in plenilunio carne sunt repleti, mare cum tumescit: humores in hominis corpore, veluti etiam in arboribus, aucti sunt. Contrarium autem in novilunio apparet. Praeterea secundum accessum et recessum Lunae in Zodiaci signis mutationes temporum fiunt. Cum enim Sol et Luna in signis calidis sunt, augetur caliditas: cum demum in frigidis, frigiditas calidum superat. Meritò igitur in humorum purgatione Lunae motus per zodiaci signa animadvertendus est. Et non Solum autem Lunae motus: verum etiam aliorum planetarum, quorum influxu humores conservantur, animadvertendus est.
translation: CHAPTER XII. How diseases, their causes, and the affected parts can be recognized has been said in the preceding. Whether they are curable or incurable, acute or chronic, and at what time they should be judged, has also been shown before. Now, however, it is appropriate to show how they are to be cured and at what time remedies should be applied. Therefore, to show the matter briefly, it must be noted that inferior bodies are ruled by superior ones, and especially by the motion of the Moon, diseases vary: not because it excels others in strength and power, but because it is nearer and swifter in motion. For we see humors increase. Bones are filled with marrow at the full moon; all bodies, both in the sea and on land, with increased humor, the Moon causes humors to increase: just as when it decreases, they diminish. Crabs, lobsters, and oysters are full of flesh at the full moon, when the sea swells: humors in the human body, as also in trees, are increased. The opposite appears at the new moon. Moreover, according to the access and recess of the Moon in the signs of the Zodiac, changes of seasons occur. For when the Sun and Moon are in hot signs, heat increases; when finally in cold ones, coldness overcomes heat. Rightly, therefore, in the purgation of humors, the Moon's motion through the signs of the zodiac must be observed. And not only the Moon's motion, but also that of other planets, by whose influence humors are preserved, must be observed.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Planetary Influence on Humors
text: Saturnus enim melancholiam auget, conservat et retinet: quia cum frigidus sit et siccus, ut melancholia: ideò eam auget et retinet. Iupiter verò qui calidus est et humidus temperate, sanguini praeest, ipsumque auget. Melancholiae autem ut Saturnus, sanguini adversatur: Mars flavam bilem regit, ac gubernat, et retinet: quia ut bilis ipsa calida est et sicca: sic Mars calidus est et siccus, ac ideò eam fovet: pituitam autem sua qualitate contraria, quemadmodum Venus cum Luna bilem expellit. Cum enim Venus et Luna frigidae sint et humidae: ideo pituitam fovent et regunt. Fortitudo igitur planetarum symbolum cum humoribus habentium, in eorum evacuatione vitanda est. Sed planetarum illis adversorum fortitudo molienda est. Ut quando evacuanda est bilis, Mars in cadenti domo (non autem in angulo vel succedenti) ponendus est. Venus autem è contrario, in angulis vel succedentibus fortificanda est. Illa enim sua qualitate contraria adversus bilem naturam adiuvat, et ad illius expulsionem facultatem expultricem roborat. Si etiam in ipsius bilis evacuatione Luna una cum Venere corroboretur, naturam ad illius expulsionem fortiorem redderet. Quod si ambae, id est Venus et Luna fortificari non possunt: debilitato Marte, una corroboretur. Quando pariter Venerem viribus Mars superaverit, pituitam depellet. Cum autem melancholiam evacuare volumus, Iupiter ut eam depellere queat, fortificandus est: ne autem eam Saturnus retineat, ipsum debiliorem reddere conamus. Contraria penitus ratio in sanguine vacuando observatur. Iupiter enim debilior redditur, et Saturni vires roborantur: ne Iupiter sanguinis vacuationem prohibeat, et Saturnus iuvet.
translation: For Saturn increases, preserves, and retains melancholy: because since he is cold and dry, like melancholy, he therefore increases and retains it. Jupiter, however, who is warm and moderately moist, presides over blood and increases it. But just as Saturn is adverse to blood, Mars rules, governs, and retains yellow bile: because just as bile itself is hot and dry, so Mars is hot and dry, and therefore fosters it. Venus with the Moon, however, expels phlegm by its contrary quality. For since Venus and the Moon are cold and moist, they therefore foster and rule phlegm. The strength of planets having a symbol with humors must therefore be avoided in their evacuation. But the strength of planets adverse to them must be worked upon. For example, when bile is to be evacuated, Mars must be placed in a cadent house (but not in an angle or succedent house). Venus, on the contrary, must be fortified in angles or succedent houses. For she, by her contrary quality, aids nature against bile and strengthens the expulsive faculty for its expulsion. If also, in the evacuation of bile, the Moon together with Venus is strengthened, it would render nature stronger for its expulsion. But if both, that is, Venus and the Moon, cannot be fortified: with Mars debilitated, one of them should be strengthened. When Mars has equally overcome Venus in strength, he will expel phlegm. But when we wish to evacuate melancholy, Jupiter must be fortified so that he can expel it: and lest Saturn retain it, we try to render him weaker. The completely opposite reasoning is observed in evacuating blood. For Jupiter is rendered weaker, and Saturn's strengths are reinforced: lest Jupiter prohibit the evacuation of blood, and Saturn help.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Electional Hours and Body Parts
text: Delectus etiam in horarum electione pariter habendus est. Iovis enim hora in melancholiae evacuatione prodest: Saturni verò nocet. ac eodem modo in singulis humoribus purgandis iudicandum est. Eo tamen tempore, quo solutivum medicamentum datur, tutius est horam benevolorum planetarum quam malevolorum eligere, ac in omnibus melius est Saturni et Martis horas, ac Lunae cum eis applicationes fugere. Planetae item corporis humani partes quasdam respiciunt: utpote eas quae naturis suis conveniunt. Ut quia Luna frigida est et humida, cerebrum respicit. Venus autem quae pariter temperate humida est testiculos conservat. Mercurius communis est et convertibilis. Ideò pulmones gubernat. Sol qui calorem conservativum adeptus est, suo influxu cor moderatur. Mars verò quoniam calidus est et siccus immoderate, vesicam felleam et renes respicit et moderatur. Hepar autem caliditate et humiditate Iovis, quemadmodum frigiditate et siccitate Saturni lien regitur. Eo igitur tempore quo illi planetae dominantur, partes illae evacuandae non sunt: humorem enim noxium retinerent, et curationem impedirent. Eo tamen tempore quo fortes ac fortunati in caelo sunt, illas partes roborare convenientissimum erit. Ad rem igitur unde digressi sumus, accedamus.
translation: A choice must also be made in the election of hours. For Jupiter's hour is beneficial in the evacuation of melancholy; but Saturn's is harmful. And in the same way, judgment must be made in purging individual humors. However, at the time when a laxative medicine is given, it is safer to choose the hour of benevolent planets than of malevolent ones, and in all cases it is better to avoid the hours of Saturn and Mars, and the Moon's applications to them. Planets also relate to certain parts of the human body, namely those that correspond to their natures. For example, because the Moon is cold and moist, it relates to the brain. Venus, which is likewise moderately moist, preserves the testicles. Mercury is common and convertible, therefore it governs the lungs. The Sun, which has acquired conservative heat, moderates the heart by its influence. Mars, however, because he is excessively hot and dry, relates to and moderates the gallbladder and kidneys. The liver is ruled by the heat and moisture of Jupiter, just as the spleen is ruled by the coldness and dryness of Saturn. Therefore, at the time when those planets dominate, those parts should not be evacuated: for they would retain harmful humor and impede healing. However, at the time when they are strong and fortunate in the sky, it will be most appropriate to strengthen those parts. Let us therefore return to the matter from which we digressed.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon Phases and Humoral Movement
text: Motum Lunae nostri corporis humores sequuntur. In prima enim et tertia Lunae hebdomadibus, hoc est à Solis et Lunae congressu sive conjunctione ad primam quadraturam, et ab oppositione sive plenilunio ad quadraturam secundam, à centro ad circumferentiam humores moventur: et ideò sanguinis missio, balnea, stupae et medicamenta quaecunque à centro ad circumferentiam humores ducere nata sunt, in his hebdomadibus conveniunt. In secunda verò et quarta Lunae hebdomadibus id est, à prima quadratura Lunae ad eius cum Sole oppositionem sive plenilunium, humores à circumferentia ad centrum moventur: et ideo medicamenta quae humores à circumferentia ad centrum ducere nata sunt, in his hebdomadibus conveniunt.
translation: The humors of our body follow the Moon's motion. For in the first and third weeks of the Moon, that is, from the conjunction of the Sun and Moon to the first quarter, and from the opposition or full moon to the second quarter, humors move from the center to the circumference: and therefore bloodletting, baths, fomentations, and whatever medicines are designed to draw humors from the center to the circumference, are suitable in these weeks. In the second and fourth weeks of the Moon, that is, from the first quarter of the Moon to its opposition with the Sun or full moon, humors move from the circumference to the center: and therefore medicines designed to draw humors from the circumference to the center are suitable in these weeks.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon's Phases for Purgation
text: Non folùm quadraturę Lunę in purgadis humoribus aduertendę funt:ſed etiam ſigna in quibus Luna eft, & eius ad planetas varios aſpectus. Purgantia medicamenta Luna in aqueis ſignis, vtpote in Cancro, Scor pione, & Piſcibus exiſtente, commodiſsimè dantur. Pręſtantior item aut deterior purgatio fit, pro diuer- ſis Lunæ ad alios atque alios planetas aſpectibus. Pil lulę etiam quibufdam téporibus magis conueniút, alio autem tempore electuaria, alio demùm potiones. Quibus autem temporibus conuenient ſeques tabula docet.
translation: Not only are the Moon's quadratures to be observed in purging humors, but also the signs in which the Moon is, and its various aspects to the planets. Purgative medicines are given most conveniently when the Moon is in watery signs, such as Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces. Likewise, purgation is better or worse depending on the Moon's aspects to various other planets. Pills are also more suitable at certain times, electuaries at another time, and finally potions at another. The following table shows which times are suitable.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon in Cancer for Purgation
text: In Cancro & cum uo tu are co * aut Δ do Luna hæc ſigna perambulans,& Veneris cofert ad bilem vacuandam Solís vel martis ad phlegmatis vacuationé pro- Cũ electuario. deft Iouis ad melancholiam educendam proficit Veneris confert bilis va cuatio
translation: In Cancer, and when the Moon traverses these signs with a sextile or trine aspect to Venus, it helps to evacuate bile. With a sextile or trine to the Sun or Mars, it is beneficial for evacuating phlegm. With a sextile or trine to Jupiter, it is effective for expelling melancholy. Venus helps to evacuate bile. (With an electuary.)

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon in Scorpio for Purgation
text: In Scorpione & cũ bens habendata lis eft humorum euacuandorum ratio. Solis aut Martis pituitæ aut aſpectu expulfionem iuuat Cum potione. Iouis melancholiæ purgationem auget Veneris, euacuare bilem confert
translation: In Scorpio, and when the Moon has these aspects, the method of evacuating humors is as follows: The Sun or Mars by aspect aids the expulsion of phlegm. Jupiter increases the purgation of melancholy. Venus helps to evacuate bile. (With a potion.)

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon in Pisces for Purgation
text: In Piſcibus cum * aut aſpectu Solisvel Martis pituitam purgare tempeftiuũ eſt Cum pillulis itione Iouis humorem melan. cholicum purgare licet
translation: In Pisces, with a sextile or trine aspect to the Sun or Mars, it is timely to purge phlegm. With a sextile or trine to Jupiter, it is permissible to purge melancholic humor. (With pills.)

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon-Jupiter and Laxatives
text: Cùm tamen ſolutiuum medicamentum exhibetur, Luna Ioui coniuncta non sit. Quamuis enim Iu piter bonus ac fortunatus sit:tamen quoniam natu rę parens est & amicus, eam corroborat, & aduerſus medicamentű adiuuat:ita vt medicamétum à natu ra ſuperatú, opus suum minimè perficit. Iuxta illud Ptholemei decimonono aphoriſmo centiloquij: Si quis Luna cum Ioue exiſtente laxatiuum acceperit,medi cine opus et effectus minuetur.
translation: However, when a laxative medicine is administered, the Moon should not be conjoined with Jupiter. For although Jupiter is good and fortunate, yet because he is a parent and friend of nature, he strengthens it and aids against the medicine, so that the medicine, overcome by nature, performs its work minimally. According to Ptolemy's nineteenth aphorism of the Centiloquy: If anyone takes a laxative when the Moon is with Jupiter, the medicine's work and effect will be diminished.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon for Vomiting
text: Quoniam autem plerunque ita vrget morbus, vt moram refpuat: cauendum est, ſi fieri poſsit, ne Luna sit in signis ruminantibus, vt in Ariete, vel Tauro, vel Capricorno, neque planetæ retrogrado applicet, vel iuncta sit: quia hęc vomitum prouocant. Illud tamen tempus in vomitiui medicamenti exhibitione cómodiſsimum est: ſed aſcendens & dominus eius roborādus est & fortunandus. Cauendum à maleuolorum aſpectu, quoad fieri potest. Dominus autem aſcendentis ſi fieri potest, vel planetæ ſub terra exiſtenti iunctus sit, aut eidem applicans, vt humores faciliùs deor- sum ducantur.
translation: Since disease often presses so urgently that it rejects delay, care must be taken, if possible, that the Moon is not in ruminant signs, such as Aries, Taurus, or Capricorn, nor applying to or conjoined with a retrograde planet, because these provoke vomiting. However, that time is most convenient for administering emetic medicine: but the Ascendant and its lord must be strengthened and made fortunate. Malefic aspects should be avoided as much as possible. The lord of the Ascendant, if possible, should be conjoined with a planet below the earth, or applying to it, so that humors may be more easily drawn downwards.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon's Light for Laxatives
text: In medicinę prętereà laxatiuæ exhibitione ſi motu & lumine Luna diminuta sit, pre- stantior erit.
translation: Furthermore, in the administration of laxative medicine, if the Moon is diminished in motion and light, it will be more effective.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon's Influence on Bodily Virtues
text: Quemadmodum autem in humorum purgatio ne Lunæ motus animaduertitur: ita in corporis facultatum fiue virium roboratione animaduertendus est hoc modo. Luna in is fignis gıdıs & ficcis In fignis aëreis ca, xistente cultates quantur. In fignis igneis ca Ariete lidis & ficcis Leone Sagittario Tauro In fignisterreis fri Virgine Capricorno ? Geminis Libra lidis & humidiıs Aquario } Cancro In fignis aqueis frigidis & humidis S plemanque et for- zer catio 0 fa ten in his Scorpione Piſcibus 103 Atractrix virtus roboratur: quoniam ſecundum loci natura Luna influit, & hæc facultas qualitatibus ſimilibus viget. Retentrix virtus tuuatur. Iuuatur enim illis qualitatibus, quæ illo tempore dominantur. Concoctrix facultas cófirmatur:talibus enim S 2 qualitatibus perficitur. Expultrix validior redditur, quæ his qualita- {tibus potifsimam iuuatur & perſicitur.
translation: Just as the Moon's motion is observed in the purgation of humors, so too it must be observed in the strengthening of the body's faculties or powers in this way: When the Moon is in hot and dry signs (Aries, Leo, Sagittarius), the attractive virtue is strengthened, because the Moon influences according to the nature of the place, and this faculty thrives on similar qualities. When the Moon is in cold and dry signs (Taurus, Virgo, Capricorn), the retentive virtue is aided, for it is helped by the qualities that dominate at that time. When the Moon is in cold and moist signs (Cancer, Scorpio, Pisces), the concoctive faculty is confirmed, for it is perfected by such qualities. When the Moon is in hot and moist signs (Gemini, Libra, Aquarius), the expulsive virtue is made stronger, which is especially aided and perfected by these qualities.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Strengthening Medicines and Moon
text: Eo igitur tempore & Luna cum Ioue aut Venere iuncta exiſtente, roborantia medicaméta &adſtringentia danda sunt.
translation: Therefore, at that time, and when the Moon is conjoined with Jupiter or Venus, strengthening and astringent medicines should be given.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Correcting Temperaments with Moon
text: Cùm auté intéperiem aliquam corrigere intendimus, Luna sit in signo ea qualitate prędito quam cupimus inducere. Vt quando calfacere volumus, ignea signa eo tempore Luna debet occupare: cùm refrigerare, frigida tenebit: cùm humectare, humida:cùm demùm siccare, sicca poſside re debet. Quæ autem ea sint precedenti tabula & primo capite primi libri maniſeſtum fuit.
translation: However, when we intend to correct some intemperance, the Moon should be in a sign endowed with the quality we wish to induce. For example, when we want to warm, the Moon should occupy fiery signs at that time; when to cool, it will hold cold signs; when to moisten, moist signs; and finally, when to dry, it should possess dry signs. What these signs are was made clear in the preceding table and the first chapter of the first book.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon in Signs for Body Parts
text: Par tes igitur quæ sunt à capite vſq; ad pectus Luna exi- stente in tribus signis supradictis, vel aſcendentibus illis, à morbo vindicantur. Et partes quæ à pectore sunt vſque ad pudenda, à morbis Luna in Cancro, Leone & virgine exiſtente, aut illis aſcendentibus, liberantur. Quæ autem sunt à pudendis vſque ad ge nua:cùm Librá, Scorpioné & Sagittarium Lunatenebit,curabuntur. Partes demùm, quæ sunt à genibus vſq; ad pedes, Luna in Capricorno, Aquario & Piſcibus exiſtente ad sanitatem reſtitundę sunt.
translation: Therefore, the parts from the head to the chest are cured of disease when the Moon is in the three aforementioned signs (Aries, Taurus, Gemini), or when those signs are ascending. And the parts from the chest to the genitals are freed from diseases when the Moon is in Cancer, Leo, and Virgo, or when those signs are ascending. Those parts from the genitals to the knees will be treated when the Moon occupies Libra, Scorpio, and Sagittarius. Finally, the parts from the knees to the feet are to be restored to health when the Moon is in Capricorn, Aquarius, and Pisces.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Surgery and Moon-Mars Aspects
text: Ca uè tamen ne parté ferro tangas, quando Luna signú hanc parAVTHO. CLAV. DARIOTO MEDI. 105 hanc partem reſpiciens tenebit ac in omnibus opera tionibus quę ferro fiunt, vide ne Luna quadratum, aut quadrati vel oppoſitionis (alij enim mediocres sunt) applicationem ad Martem habeat.
translation: However, be careful not to touch a part with iron when the Moon occupies the sign ruling that part, and in all operations performed with iron, see that the Moon does not have an application to Mars by square, or by square or opposition (for other aspects are mediocre).

author: Claude Dariot
title: Houses and Lords for Treatment
text: Non solùm autem signa, verumetiam domus animaduertendæ sunt. Quéadmodum enim de signis dictum est, ita domus in corporis humani partesinfluunt. Cùm ergo morbi alicuius partis curatio incipitur: domus hanc partem reſpiciens, & eius dominus benè affecti sint & fortunati. Veluti cum capitis ægritudo curan da est:prima domus & eius dominus (quem almutem Arabi vocant) beneuolorú aſpectu vel pręſentia, roborandi sunt, ac fortunadi, si futuram curationem profperam esse cupimus. quéadmodum secunda domus & eius dominus, si collum:tertia si humeros, manus & brachia: quarta si pulmones, pectus & mammillas ad priſtinum salutis gradum reducere optes.ita enim de singulis domibus & earum dominis faciendum, cùm partes his domibus subiecta fanitati reſtituere velimus. In omnium ergo curationum principio aſcendens eius dominus, domus partem reſpiciens& eius dominus fortunandi sunt.
translation: Not only signs, but also houses must be observed. For just as it has been said about signs, so houses influence parts of the human body. Therefore, when the treatment of a disease of any part is begun, the house ruling that part and its lord should be well-affected and fortunate. For example, when a head ailment is to be treated: the first house and its lord (whom the Arabs call Almuten) should be strengthened and made fortunate by the aspect or presence of benefics, if we wish the future cure to be prosperous. Likewise for the second house and its lord, if you wish to restore the neck; the third, if the shoulders, hands, and arms; the fourth, if the lungs, chest, and breasts to their former state of health. Thus it must be done for each house and its lord, when we wish to restore to health the parts subject to these houses. Therefore, at the beginning of all treatments, the Ascendant, its lord, the house ruling the part, and its lord must be made fortunate.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Jupiter's Placement for Healing
text: Iupiter autem ſi fieri potest, in aſcendente vel vndecima domo, vel decima, aut nona ponendus est, ac bonitate augedus.
translation: Jupiter, if possible, should be placed in the Ascendant, or the eleventh house, or the tenth, or the ninth, and his beneficence should be increased.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Fortifying Planet Ruling Disease
text: Planeta similiter morbum quem curare volumus dominans & partem respiciens, fortunandus est, vt si phrenitis curanda sit, Mercurius Lunam trino aut sextili aſpectu aſpiciet : & optimè ac in bono loco collocabitur, ac in suis dignitatibus:vt (exempli gratia) si in septem primis gradıbus virginis, in sua domo, exaltatione & terminis erit. Si aute frigidam & humidam ventriculi intemperiem corrigere velis Venus fortunanda est & corroboranda, vt si in octo primis Tauri gradibus, vel in duodecim primis Piſciu ponatur, in sua domo vel exaltatione & terminis erit : vel si in Libra ponatur in sua domo etiam erit.
translation: Similarly, the planet ruling the disease we wish to cure and ruling the part, must be made fortunate. For example, if phrenitis is to be cured, Mercury will aspect the Moon with a trine or sextile aspect; and he will be placed optimally and in a good place, and in his dignities: for example, if he is in the first seven degrees of Virgo, in his own house, exaltation, and terms. If, however, you wish to correct a cold and moist intemperance of the stomach, Venus must be made fortunate and strengthened, so that if she is placed in the first eight degrees of Taurus, or the first twelve of Pisces, she will be in her own house or exaltation and terms; or if placed in Libra, she will also be in her own house.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Contrasting Rules for Purgation
text: Patet ergo quòd in humorum purgatione & morborum curatione, contraria in plane tis & signis ratio seruatur. Nam in alicuius humoris purgatione fortitudo planetæ simbolum habentis cum humore purgando vitatur: sed signum fimbolum cum eo habens eligitur. In euacuatione bilis, signum calidum & ficcum ac partem reſpiciens eligitur:tempúſq; quo Luna ipſo sit expectatur, vel aſcédere cogitur.Signa verò frigida & humida prophlegmatis vacuatione coſerunt. Frigida verò &sicca in melancholico humore purgando: calida denique & humida in sanguine extrahendo eliguntur. Illa autem quæ sint in precedentibus satis maniſeſtum fuit.
translation: It is clear, therefore, that in the purgation of humors and the cure of diseases, a contrary rule is observed for planets and signs. For in the purgation of any humor, the strength of the planet symbolizing the humor to be purged is avoided; but the sign symbolizing it is chosen. In the evacuation of bile, a hot and dry sign ruling the part is chosen, and the time when the Moon is in it is awaited, or it is forced to ascend. Cold and moist signs are suitable for the evacuation of phlegm. Cold and dry signs for purging melancholic humor; and finally, hot and moist signs are chosen for extracting blood. What these are was sufficiently clear in the preceding.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Bloodletting - Moon Phases by Age
text: Quòd itaque ad aſtro.. rum motus attinet, motus Lunæ &ipsius ad alios pla netas aſpectus precipuè attédendi sunt. Luua enim in quolibet planetarum aſpectu exiſtente, sanguinis detractio non bona est. Primò igitur quòd ad Lunæ motum attinet, sciendum est tempus quod à Solis& Lunæ coniunctione, id est à nouilunio vſque ad primam Lunæ quadraturam aptum esse iuuenibus pro sanguinis miſsione. A' prima autem quadratura siue quarta vſque ad Solis & Lunę oppoſitionem siue pleniluniű, sanguinis miſsio iuuenibus ac virilis couenit. Poſt oppoſitionem siue plenilunium ad secun dam Lunæ quadratură, sanguis ætate florentibus ac senibus mittédus est. A' secunda deniq; Lunæ quadraturavſq; ad nouiluniű, venę sectio senibus couenit.
translation: Therefore, as far as the movements of the stars are concerned, the movements of the Moon and its aspects to other planets must be especially observed. For when the Moon is in any planetary aspect, bloodletting is not good. First, as far as the Moon's motion is concerned, it must be known that the time from the conjunction of the Sun and Moon, that is, from the new moon up to the Moon's first quarter, is suitable for young people for bloodletting. From the first quarter up to the opposition of the Sun and Moon, or full moon, bloodletting is suitable for young men and those in their prime. After the opposition or full moon to the second quarter of the Moon, blood should be drawn from those in the prime of life and the elderly. Finally, from the second quarter of the Moon up to the new moon, venesection is suitable for the elderly.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Bloodletting - Moon in Signs by Temperament
text: In omnibus tamen cóplexionibusvenę sectio minimè cópetit, Luna in omnibus signis exiſtente. Sed pituitofis,cùm erit in Ariete & Sagittario conuenit: cephalica tamen in Ariete excepta:in Sagittario autem coxarum vena. Melancholicis auté cum Aquarium & primam Librę medietaté Luna tenebit, sanguinis miſsio non prohibetur. Aquarius tamen tibias, Libra nates excipiet. Cùm demùm Cancrú, vel piſces Luna peragrabit, sanguinis vacuatio biliofis conferet. Cancer tamen pulmonum venam, Piſces pedú incidere prohibet. Iuxta illud Ptholemei apho rismo vigefimo. Tangere partem ferro Luna exiſtente in signo partem hanc reſpiciente periculosum est.
translation: However, bloodletting is least suitable for all complexions when the Moon is in all signs. But for phlegmatic individuals, it is suitable when the Moon is in Aries and Sagittarius; however, the cephalic vein in Aries is excepted, and in Sagittarius, the veins of the hips. For melancholic individuals, when the Moon occupies Aquarius and the first half of Libra, bloodletting is not prohibited. However, Aquarius excepts the shins, and Libra the buttocks. Finally, when the Moon traverses Cancer or Pisces, blood evacuation will benefit bilious individuals. However, Cancer prohibits cutting the vein of the lungs, and Pisces prohibits cutting the veins of the feet. According to Ptolemy's twentieth aphorism: It is dangerous to touch a part with iron when the Moon is in the sign ruling that part.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Bloodletting - Unsuitable Signs
text: Alia autem signa nempè Leo, Gemini,vltima Libræ medietas & Scorpius, ad sanguinis miſsione apta non sunt. Leo, scilicet propter calore, & quia Solis domus est. Gemini quoniam brachia reſpiciút, in quibusvt plu rimum vena inciditur. Vltima Libræ medietas:quia via est combuſta, & ibi etiam Luna (quę ab infortunijs libera esse debet) infortunatur. Scorpius deniq; quia via unz pri po AVTHO. CLAV. DARIOTO MEDI. 109 quia via combuſta cum vltima Librę medietate vocatur: & quia Martis domus est, venę sectionem vetat. Triplicitas verò terrea non conuenit, ac rarò eligitur:nisi in curationis morbi antiqui principio, vt iam dictum est in precedentibus.
translation: Other signs, namely Leo, Gemini, the last half of Libra, and Scorpio, are not suitable for bloodletting. Leo, namely, because of its heat, and because it is the Sun's house. Gemini, because it rules the arms, in which the vein is most often cut. The last half of Libra: because it is the Via Combusta, and there the Moon (which should be free from misfortunes) is also afflicted. Finally, Scorpio, because it is called the Via Combusta with the last half of Libra; and because it is Mars's house, it forbids venesection. The earthy triplicity is not suitable and is rarely chosen, except at the beginning of the treatment of an ancient disease, as has already been said in the preceding.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Bloodletting - Unfavorable Moon Aspects
text: Lunę prętereà ad alios atque alios planetas diuerſi aſpectus, hanc fieri impediunt vel iuuent. Qui autem hanc fieri vetant, hîc ordine sequuntur. Lunæ cum Sole Miſsionem sanguinis ante & poft trium dierum spacio impedit. Sanguinis vacuationem per medium diem ab vtraque parte prohibet. Venæ apertionem diem vnum ante & poſt vetat. Lunæ aut Diem vnum ante & poſt sanguinis mifsionem impedit. Infortunato Lunæ cum Infortunata Diei vnius spacio ab vtraque parte venam aperire prohibet. Venę item apertio Luna curſu vacua exiſtente non fiat:neque hoc tempore purgans medicamentū dandum est. Ab hora malorum Saturni, scilicet vel Martis, in sanguinis vacuatione cauédum est. Luna denique eo tempore quo aperitur vena, signa communia si fieri poſsit, no teneat: actionem inim impediunt, & sanguinis fluxum ob erroré pariunt.
translation: Furthermore, various aspects of the Moon to other planets hinder or help this to be done. Those that forbid it are listed here in order. Moon with Sun: hinders bloodletting for a space of three days before and after. Prohibits blood evacuation for half a day on either side. Forbids opening a vein one day before and after. Moon with Saturn or Mars: hinders bloodletting one day before and after. Moon with a malefic: prohibits opening a vein for a space of one day on either side. Likewise, opening a vein should not be done when the Moon is Void of Course; nor should purgative medicine be given at this time. Care must be taken during the hour of the malefics, namely Saturn or Mars, in blood evacuation. Finally, when a vein is opened, the Moon, if possible, should not occupy common signs, for they hinder the action and cause a flow of blood due to error.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Bloodletting - Favorable Moon Aspects
text: Nunc qui- nam Lunæ ad planetas aſpectus mif sionem sanguinis laudabilem faciunt dicendum est. Sunt autem que sequuntur. pij Ho DE MORBIS ET DIEBVS CRITIC. Afpectus laudabiles in sanguinis mißione. Lunæ cum Optima est pro sanguinis miſsione, modo ta. men Venus combuſta non sit. Lunæ vel cum Bonus est in sanguinis vacuatione, cùm autem & optimus. Lunæ quadratus cum In sanguine mit conuenit: bona est. Oppositio cum eifdem Mediocres in phlebotomia funt. Illi igitur afpectus diligenter animaduertendi sunt, si miſsionem sanguinis prosperam ac salubrem fore speramus. Denique cauendum est in sanguine mittendo, ne octauę domus siue mortis domino Lu na applicet.
translation: Now it must be said which aspects of the Moon to the planets make bloodletting commendable. These are the following: Commendable aspects in bloodletting. Moon with Venus: is optimal for bloodletting, provided, however, that Venus is not combust. Moon with Jupiter or Sun: is good for blood evacuation, and indeed optimal. Moon square Jupiter or Venus: is suitable for bloodletting; it is good. Opposition with the same: are mediocre in phlebotomy. Therefore, these aspects must be diligently observed if we hope for a prosperous and healthy bloodletting. Finally, care must be taken in bloodletting that the Moon does not apply to the lord of the eighth house, or house of death.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Baths - Moon Aspects
text: Balneum adhuc Lunæ ad maleuolos planetas aſpectus, impediunt : vt Lunæ & vel aut ad Saturnum aut Martem. Lunę prętereà aſpectus aut applicationes cum domi no octauę domus & planetis cobuſtis balneum impediunt. Lunæ autem ad beneuolos planetas aspe- êtus & applicationes, vt eius coniunctio aut sextilis aut trinus aſpectus aut eorundem aſpectuum applicationes ad Iouem vel Venerem, aut trinus vel sexti lis ad Solem: vel etiam quadratus & oppoſitio Lunæ ad Iouem & Venerem Balneum preſtantius ac tempus ad balneum conuenientiſsimum reddunt.
translation: Furthermore, aspects of the Moon to malefic planets hinder bathing: such as the Moon's conjunction, square, or opposition to Saturn or Mars. Moreover, aspects or applications of the Moon with the lord of the eighth house and combust planets hinder bathing. However, aspects and applications of the Moon to benefic planets, such as its conjunction or sextile or trine aspect, or applications of the same aspects to Jupiter or Venus, or a trine or sextile to the Sun; or even a square and opposition of the Moon to Jupiter and Venus, make the bath more effective and the time most suitable for bathing.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Cupping - Moon in Signs and Aspects
text: Iam verò de cucurbitulis sermone facturis dicamus eas non debere membro alicui apponi: Luna exiſtente in signo cui pars illa siue membrum subiacet. Iuxta illud Ptholemei iam dictum aphorismovigefimo:vt plurimum enim cum facrificatione apponútur. Lunę pariter ad malos aſpectus cucurbitularum appoſi tionem prohibent, vt Lunæ coniunctio, quadratus aut oppoſitio cum Marte vel Saturno, aut eorúdem aſpectuum applicationes. Luna item per alia atque alia signa discurrente complexionibus quibufdam conueniunt. Veluti pituitofis Luna in Ariete & Sagittario exiſtente, cóuenientiſsimè dentur. Excepto tamen capite in Ariete, exceptiſq; coxis in Sagitta rio. Bilioſis autem Luna per Cancrum, Scorpionem & Piſces discurrente, commodè apponútur. Cancer verò pectus excipit, Scorpius verenda & femora, Pi- sces deniq; pedes prohibent. Melancholicis demùm cùm in Aquariovel Libra Luna fuerit affiguntur:na tes tamé in Libra,tibię in Aquario prohibetur. Hęcque omnia optima habentur, quando ad beneuolos planetas aſpectus aut applicationes Luna habuerit. Veluti Lunæ coniunctio, sextilis aut trinus aſpectus vel eorundem aſpectum applicationes ad Iouemvel Venerem, aut etiam quadratus & oppoſitio eorundem, idonea cucurbitularum appoſitioné pręſtant.
translation: Now, concerning cupping glasses, let us say that they should not be applied to any limb when the Moon is in the sign to which that part or limb is subject. According to Ptolemy's already mentioned twentieth aphorism: for they are mostly applied with sacrifice. Likewise, bad aspects of the Moon prohibit the application of cupping glasses, such as the Moon's conjunction, square, or opposition with Mars or Saturn, or applications of the same aspects. Also, as the Moon traverses different signs, it is suitable for certain complexions. For phlegmatic individuals, for example, they are most conveniently given when the Moon is in Aries and Sagittarius, except for the head in Aries, and the hips in Sagittarius. For bilious individuals, however, they are conveniently applied when the Moon traverses Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces. But Cancer excepts the chest, Scorpio the genitals and thighs, and Pisces finally prohibits the feet. For melancholic individuals, finally, they are affixed when the Moon is in Aquarius or Libra; however, the buttocks in Libra and the shins in Aquarius are prohibited. All these are considered optimal when the Moon has aspects or applications to benefic planets. For example, the Moon's conjunction, sextile, or trine aspect, or applications of the same aspects to Jupiter or Venus, or even their square and opposition, provide for a suitable application of cupping glasses.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Enemas - Moon in Signs and Aspects
text: In ænematis autem iniectione Scorpioné vel Libram Luna teneat,ac planetæ sub terra exiſtēti applicet. Ad Iouem prętereà vel Venerem sextilem aut trinum, vel etiam quadratum aut oppoſitionem Lu na habeat, aut eiſdem coniuncta sit. Sed cauendum est ne aſcendentis dominus domino sextę iungatur, ne ei aliquo aſpectu applicet. A'malorú aſpectu Lu na & dominus aſcendentis summoperè arcédi funt.
translation: In the injection of enemas, the Moon should occupy Scorpio or Libra, and apply to a planet existing below the earth. Furthermore, the Moon should have a sextile or trine, or even a square or opposition to Jupiter or Venus, or be conjoined with them. But care must be taken that the lord of the Ascendant is not conjoined with the lord of the sixth, nor applies to it by any aspect. The Moon and the lord of the Ascendant must be especially kept away from malefic aspects.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Topical Remedies and Moon's Light
text: Topica verò remedia parti apponi debent. Luna in signo huic parti preſidenti exiſtente. Veluti si capiti topica apponantur, Luna Arietem teneat. si autem brachijs, Geminos poſsideat:ac sic in alijs. Eo ta men tempore, neque ignis, neque ferrum apponantur, iuxta prędicta. Si autem remedia carnem generantia adhibeantur:aucta lumine & numero, aut in sui incremento Luna sit. Cùm demùm carnem minuentia remedia adhibentur, contraria ratio seruatur: eo enim tempore quo Luna lumine & numero diminuta est, debent apponi. Refrigerentia deniq; calfacientia & alias qualitates inducentia medicamenta, Luna his qualitatibus cóuenientia signa di- scurrente, apponi debent.
translation: Topical remedies should be applied to the part when the Moon is in the sign ruling that part. For example, if topical remedies are applied to the head, the Moon should occupy Aries. If to the arms, it should possess Gemini; and so on for others. However, at that time, neither fire nor iron should be applied, according to what has been said. If, however, remedies that generate flesh are used, the Moon should be increased in light and number, or in its increment. When, finally, remedies that reduce flesh are used, the opposite rule is observed: for they should be applied at the time when the Moon is diminished in light and number. Finally, cooling, warming, and other quality-inducing medicines should be applied when the Moon traverses signs suitable for these qualities.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Planetary Rulership of Illness Causes
text: exercitatio immoderata, otium &labor. Quo pacto autem causae cognosci possint antea docuimus. Curatio secundum causarum diversitatem variatur. Causae vero a planetis quibusdam illis qualitatibus convenientibus reguntur, ut sanguis a Iove: bilis vero cum caliditate a Marte gubernatur: sic etiam aliae causae a planetis aliis reguntur. Illud tamen non est praetereundum, spiritus & vapores a Mercurio regi & gubernari.
translation: Immoderate exercise, leisure & labor. How the causes can be known, we have taught before. The cure varies according to the diversity of causes. Indeed, causes are ruled by certain planets with qualities corresponding to them, as blood by Jupiter: bile, however, with heat, is governed by Mars: so also other causes are ruled by other planets. This, however, must not be omitted, that spirits & vapors are ruled & governed by Mercury. Therefore, when someone is about to undertake the cure of these diseases, let him choose a suitable celestial influence. Let the Moon, therefore, increased in light & number, & free from misfortunes, be placed in Aries. Care must be taken, however, that it does not ascend: for in the ascendant, as has been said, it is afflicted by misfortune. But let the lord of the ascendant, because the ascendant regards the head, be well-fortified, and free from evils: nor let there be malefics in the ascendant. Care must also be taken that the Moon, especially in its increase, is not joined to Mars by square or opposition or even by conjunction, and in its decrease, by the aforesaid aspects with Saturn: for Mars increases heat: Saturn, however, coldness. Mars, moreover, is harmful to the eyes in all things. If, however, the Moon in its decrease has a trine or sextile to Mars, or the same in its increase to Saturn: the cure will not be delayed on that account. Also, the hour of Mars must be avoided at the beginning of the cure, and care must be taken that it does not hold the ascendant. Finally, the cure (if possible) should begin with the Moon increasing in light and number. These things thus considered, if the disease has its cause in a peccant humor either in the whole body, or in a part or the head. With the Moon existing in Aries, or in Pisces, with Aries ascending, and the planet regarding the peccant humor being weak, it will be purged: provided, however, that the planet which is adverse to this humor is strong. After the body is purged, topical remedies should be applied in the suitable constellation mentioned above. Let the Moon, however, hold Aries, or a mutable sign, or a common sign, when fire or iron is to be applied to the eye. But if Aries ascends, a more excellent operation will be performed. When the disease is cured, with the Moon existing in the same sign and free from malefics, the head & eye should be strengthened. As for the eradication of ear diseases, it must be said that in the election of celestial influence, it differs nothing from the cure of eye diseases, because the whole head and its parts are subject to the lord of Aries. In this, however, they differ, that because the ears are drier, harder & more robust parts than the eyes, they require stronger medicaments. Therefore, since enough has been said about the cure of diseases of those parts: let us turn our pen to the cure of fevers. Teaching what fever is, and how many differences of fevers there are: and that from Galen and good authors. Fever, therefore, is heat beyond nature, which, taking its beginning from the heart, diffuses through the arteries into the whole body, and injures its actions: which others also define thus. Fever is heat beyond nature diffused from the heart into the whole body. The difference of fevers, although it can be taken either from essence or from accidents (as is clear from Galen's books on the differences of fevers). That, however, which is sought from the matter in which the heat is kindled, is common & ordinary. Therefore, since there are three things in the body (as pleased Hippocrates in the sixth book of Epidemics), namely, the containing, the contained, & the impelling, that is, solid parts, humors & spirits. For either heat beyond nature is kindled in the spirits: & then it is ephemera, which is interpreted as diaria: & it is twofold, one, namely, simple, whose access is terminated in one day, that is, in the space of twenty-four hours, & this is properly called ephemera. The other, however, whose access extends to the third day or beyond to the fourth, namely, or the fifth or sixth or even to the seventh. Or immoderate heat beyond nature is kindled in putrefying humors, & it is the second kind of fevers. Furthermore, humors putrefy in two ways. For either it putrefies within the vessels or outside: within the vessels again in two ways: namely, either all equally putrefy in the largest vessels, & then it is sinochus or putrid continens: or only one humor, & it is syneches to the Greeks, but called continua by the Latins. And it is threefold, because either yellow bile putrefies, & it is continuous tertian, so called because, no less than intermittent tertian, it has an access every third day: or phlegm acquires heat beyond nature, & putrefies, & it is continuous quotidian: or melancholy, & it is continuous quartan: because although it is continuous: nevertheless, just as tertian and quotidian have exacerbations every third day and every single day, so also quartan has an access every fourth day. Now, however, let us return to another kind of putrid fevers. Humors putrefy outside the vessels & intermittent fevers occur, as if yellow bile putrefies, it is intermittent tertian, exquisite & simple if the bile is sincere & pure: but notha or spurious, if it is mixed with phlegm. Quotidian, however, occurs when sweet phlegm putrefies, & it is called amphemeros, because it has an access every single day. If, however, glassy phlegm putrefies, it is hepiala. If finally melancholy acquires putrilage outside the vessels, it is intermittent quartan. The third kind of simple fevers is when heat beyond nature is kindled in the solid parts of the body: & then it is hectic: which if it has so increased that it wastes the solid parts & humors, it is called marasmus, & let these things briefly said about the differences of fevers suffice. However, each species of these fevers is ruled by a certain planet, whose strength in the cure of that fever must be avoided, lest it retard the cure. Which planets these are, the following table, embracing all the differences of fevers, teaches. In the cure, therefore, of these fevers, it must be diligently observed that the planet ruling and moderating the disease is not strong in the sky: but rather weak, and the planet opposing and adverse to the disease is strong and fortunate. As in the beginning of the cure of quartan fever. The applications of the Moon to Saturn, and the strength of Saturn are to be feared. Likewise, Saturn should neither be the lord of the ascendant, nor placed in it or in the midheaven. However, Jupiter, who (as said before) is adverse to it, must be strengthened and made fortunate in the ascendant or midheaven, that is, in the tenth house. And if he could rule the ascendant, a more excellent operation would be performed. But if this cannot be done, let him regard the ascendant and its lord. Let the Moon also aspect him with a trine or sextile aspect, or apply to him with these aspects. Finally, let the Moon be free from evils and misfortunes, and increased in light and number in its increment. Therefore, one must proceed in this way in the cure of the remaining fevers. As in the cure of tertian fever, Venus & Moon must be strengthened and made fortunate. The weaker Mars is, the more prosperous the cure will be. In the cure of quotidian fever, however, one must proceed in the contrary order. The Moon, however, must always be fortunate. If anyone diligently observes these things, the cure will succeed only prosperously and happily (with the favor of God, the best and greatest). Fragment of diseases & critical days to be known from the motion of the stars by Claudius Dariotus Pomarsensis, END. TO THE JUDGMENTS OF THE STARS. (This last part is a colophon and not part of the astrological rules.)

author: Claude Dariot
title: Election for Medical Treatment

Cauendum tamen est ne ascendat: in ascendente enim, ut dictum est, infortunio afficitur. Dominus autem ascendentis: quia ascendens caput respicit, bene fortunatus sit, ac liber a malis: nec in ascendente mali sint.
