(This last part is a colophon and not part of the astrological rules.)

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon Aspects for Treatment
text: Cauendum etiam est ne Luna praecipue in sui incremento cum Marte quadrato vel oppositione aut etiam corpore iuncta sit, & in sui decremento supradictis aspectibus cum Saturno: Mars enim caliditatem auget: Saturnus vero frigiditatem. Mars praeterea oculis in omnibus noxius est. Si tamen Luna in sui decremento trinum aut sextilem ad Martem, aut eosdem in sui incremento ad Saturnum habuerit: curatio ob id non remorabitur.
translation: Care must also be taken that the Moon, especially in its increase, is not joined to Mars by square or opposition or even by conjunction, and in its decrease, by the aforesaid aspects with Saturn: for Mars increases heat: Saturn, however, coldness. Mars, moreover, is harmful to the eyes in all things. If, however, the Moon in its decrease has a trine or sextile to Mars, or the same in its increase to Saturn: the cure will not be delayed on that account.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Mars Hour and Moon Phase
text: Hora etiam Martis in curationis principio fugienda est, & cauendum ne ascendentem teneat. Cura demum (si fieri possit) incipi debet Luna lumine & numero crescente.
translation: Also, the hour of Mars must be avoided at the beginning of the cure, and care must be taken that it does not hold the ascendant. Finally, the cure (if possible) should begin with the Moon increasing in light and number.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Purging Humors & Eye Treatment
text: His ita consideratis, si morbus humorem peccantem vel in toto corpore, vel in parte aut capite, causam habeat. Luna existente in Ariete, vel in Piscibus ascendente Ariete, & planeta humorem peccantem respiciens debilis existente purgabitur: modo tamen planeta, qui huic humori adversatur, fortis sit. Corpore purgato topica in debita constellatione supradicta adhibeatur. Luna tamen Arietem, aut mobile signum, vel commune teneat, quando ignis aut ferrum oculo apponendus est. Sed si Aries ascendat, praestantior operatio fiet. Curato morbo Luna in eodem signo existente ac a malis libera, caput & oculus corroborentur.
translation: These things thus considered, if the disease has its cause in a peccant humor either in the whole body, or in a part or the head. With the Moon existing in Aries, or in Pisces, with Aries ascending, and the planet regarding the peccant humor being weak, it will be purged: provided, however, that the planet which is adverse to this humor is strong. After the body is purged, topical remedies should be applied in the suitable constellation mentioned above. Let the Moon, however, hold Aries, or a mutable sign, or a common sign, when fire or iron is to be applied to the eye. But if Aries ascends, a more excellent operation will be performed. When the disease is cured, with the Moon existing in the same sign and free from malefics, the head & eye should be strengthened.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Ear Disease Treatment
text: Quod autem ad morborum aurium profligationem attinet, dicendum est, quod in caeli influentiae electione nihil ab oculorum curatione differt, quia totum caput ac eius partes Arietis domino summittuntur. In hoc tamen differunt, quod quia aures, partes oculis sicciores, duriores & robustiores sunt, medicamenta etiam valentiora optent. Quare cum de morborum illarum partium curatione satis dictum sit: ad febrium curationem stilum convertamus. Docendo quid sit febris, ac quot sunt febrium differentiae: idque ex Galeno ac bonis auctoribus.
translation: As for the eradication of ear diseases, it must be said that in the election of celestial influence, it differs nothing from the cure of eye diseases, because the whole head and its parts are subject to the lord of Aries. In this, however, they differ, that because the ears are drier, harder & more robust parts than the eyes, they require stronger medicaments. Therefore, since enough has been said about the cure of diseases of those parts: let us turn our pen to the cure of fevers. Teaching what fever is, and how many differences of fevers there are: and that from Galen and good authors.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Definition and Types of Fever
text: Febris itaque est calor praeter naturam, qui a corde initium sumens, per arterias in omne corpus diffunditur, actionesque eiusdem laedit: quam etiam alii sic finiunt. Febris est calor praeter naturam e corde in omne corpus diffusus. Differentia autem febrium, etsi vel ab essentia, vel ab accidentibus (ut patet ex Galeni libris de febrium differentiis) sumi possit. Ea tamen quae a materia in (quo calor accenditur) petitur, vulgaris est & communis. Itaque quum tria sint in corpore (ut Hippocrati libro sexto epidemiorum placuit) Continentia nempe, contenta & impetum facientia
translation: Fever, therefore, is heat beyond nature, which, taking its beginning from the heart, diffuses through the arteries into the whole body, and injures its actions: which others also define thus. Fever is heat beyond nature diffused from the heart into the whole body. The difference of fevers, although it can be taken either from essence or from accidents (as is clear from Galen's books on the differences of fevers). That, however, which is sought from the matter in which the heat is kindled, is common & ordinary. Therefore, since there are three things in the body (as pleased Hippocrates in the sixth book of Epidemics), namely, the containing, the contained, & the impelling

author: Claude Dariot
title: Fever Classification: Ephemera
text: facientia: hoc est partes solidae, humores & spiritus. Vel enim calor praeter naturam in spiritibus accenditur: & tunc fit ephemera, quae diaria interpretatur: eaque duplex est, una scilicet simplex, cuius accessio uno die, id est vigintiquatuor horarum spatio terminatur, & ea proprie ephemera, vocatur. Altera autem, cuius accessio ad tertium usque diem vel ultra ad quartum nempe, vel quintum vel sextum aut etiam ad septimum protenditur.
translation: impelling: that is, solid parts, humors & spirits. For either heat beyond nature is kindled in the spirits: & then it is ephemera, which is interpreted as diaria: & it is twofold, one, namely, simple, whose access is terminated in one day, that is, in the space of twenty-four hours, & this is properly called ephemera. The other, however, whose access extends to the third day or beyond to the fourth, namely, or the fifth or sixth or even to the seventh.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Fever Classification: Humoral
text: Aut in humoribus putrescentibus calor immodicus praeter naturam accenditur, & fit secundum febrium genus. Porro humores bifariam putrescunt. Vel enim putrefcit intra vasa aut extra: intra vasa rursus dupliciter: nempe vel omnes aequaliter in magnis potissimum vasis putrescunt, & tunc sit sinochus siue continens putris: vel unus duntaxat humor, & sit syneches Graecis, Latinis autem continua dicitur. Estque ea triplex, quoniam vel bilis flava putrescit, & sit tertiana continua, sic dicta quod non secus ac tertiana intermittens, tertio quoque die accessionem habeat: vel pituita calorem praeter naturam acquirit, & putrescit, ac sit quotidiana continua: vel melancholia, & sit continua quartana: eo quod quamvis continua sit: tamen ut tertiana tertio quoque die, & quotidiana singulis diebus exacerbationem habent ita etiam quartana quarto quoque die accessionem habet.
translation: Or immoderate heat beyond nature is kindled in putrefying humors, & it is the second kind of fevers. Furthermore, humors putrefy in two ways. For either it putrefies within the vessels or outside: within the vessels again in two ways: namely, either all equally putrefy in the largest vessels, & then it is sinochus or putrid continens: or only one humor, & it is syneches to the Greeks, but called continua by the Latins. And it is threefold, because either yellow bile putrefies, & it is continuous tertian, so called because, no less than intermittent tertian, it has an access every third day: or phlegm acquires heat beyond nature, & putrefies, & it is continuous quotidian: or melancholy, & it is continuous quartan: because although it is continuous: nevertheless, just as tertian and quotidian have exacerbations every third day and every single day, so also quartan has an access every fourth day.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Fever Classification: Intermittent
text: Iam vero ad aliud genus de febrium putridarum redeamus. Humores extra vasa putrescunt ac febres intermittentes fiunt, ut si bilis flava putrescat, sit tertiana intermittens, exquisita & simplex si sincera & pura bilis fuerit: notha vero siue spuria, si mixta cum pituita sit. Quotidiana autem sit putrescente pituita dulci, & dicitur amphemeros
translation: Now, however, let us return to another kind of putrid fevers. Humors putrefy outside the vessels & intermittent fevers occur, as if yellow bile putrefies, it is intermittent tertian, exquisite & simple if the bile is sincere & pure: but notha or spurious, if it is mixed with phlegm. Quotidian, however, occurs when sweet phlegm putrefies, & it is called amphemeros

author: Claude Dariot
title: Fever Classification: Hectic
text: dicta quod singulo quoque die accessionem habeat. Si autem pituita vitrea putrescat, sit hepiala. Si demum melancholia extra vasa putrilaginem acquirat, sit intermittens quartana. Tertium vero simplicium febrium genus est, cum in partibus solidis corporis calor praeter naturam accenditur: & tunc sit hectica: quae si ita increverit, ut partes solidas ac humores colliquat, marasmus appellatur, atque haec breviter de febrium differentiis dicta sufficiant.
translation: said to have an access every single day. If, however, glassy phlegm putrefies, it is hepiala. If finally melancholy acquires putrilage outside the vessels, it is intermittent quartan. The third kind of simple fevers is when heat beyond nature is kindled in the solid parts of the body: & then it is hectic: which if it has so increased that it wastes the solid parts & humors, it is called marasmus, & let these things briefly said about the differences of fevers suffice.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Planetary Rulers of Fevers
text: Quaelibet autem ipsarum febrium species a quodam planetarum regitur, cuius fortitudo in ipsius febris curatione vitanda est, ne curationem retardet. Qui autem sint illi planetae sequens tabula, omnes febrium differentias complectens docet.
translation: However, each species of these fevers is ruled by a certain planet, whose strength in the cure of that fever must be avoided, lest it retard the cure. Which planets these are, the following table, embracing all the differences of fevers, teaches.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Simple Ephemera Fever Rulership
text: Ephimera simpliciter & citra adjectionem. Diaria plurium dierum, quae alio nomine sinochus non putrida dicitur. Atque illae diriguntur a Iove.
translation: Simple ephemera without addition. Diaria of many days, which by another name is called non-putrid sinochus. And these are directed by Jupiter.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Putrid Sinochus Fever Rulership
text: Spiritibus, & sit ephemera, id est, diaria. estque duplex: Omnes aequaliter, & sit sinochus siue continens putrida, quae Saturni, Martis & Veneris imperio regitur.
translation: In spirits, and it is ephemera, that is, diaria. And it is twofold: All equally, and it is sinochus or putrid continens, which is ruled by the empire of Saturn, Mars & Venus.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Continuous Tertian Fever Rulership
text: Bilis flava sit tertiana continua, ardens notata Marte
translation: Yellow bile, let it be continuous tertian, ardent, noted by Mars.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Continuous Quotidian Fever Rulership
text: Pituita quotidiana continua, quae reguntur a Venere & Luna
translation: Continuous quotidian phlegm, which is ruled by Venus & Moon.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Continuous Quartan Fever Rulership
text: Melancholia quartana continua Saturno.
translation: Continuous quartan melancholy by Saturn.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Pure Intermittent Tertian Rulership
text: Pura sit tertiana exquisita, flava bilis Marte
translation: Let pure yellow bile be exquisite tertian, by Mars.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Spurious Intermittent Fever Rulership
text: Pituitae mixta sit spuria. Quae reguntur a Marte & Venere.
translation: Let mixed phlegm be spurious. Which are ruled by Mars & Venus.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Intermittent Quotidian Fever Rulership
text: Dulcis, & sit quotidiana intermittens. Utraque Veneris & Lunae imperio paret.
translation: Sweet, & let it be intermittent quotidian. Both obey the empire of Venus & Moon.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Hepiala Fever Rulership
text: Pituita vel Vitrea, & sit hepiala: Melancholia, & sit quartana intermittens, quae Saturno subjacet.
translation: Glassy phlegm, & let it be hepiala: Melancholy, & let it be intermittent quartan, which is subject to Saturn.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Intermittent Quartan Fever Rulership
text: Melancholia, & sit quartana intermittens, quae Saturno subjacet.
translation: Melancholy, & let it be intermittent quartan, which is subject to Saturn.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Hectic and Marasmus Fever Rulership
text: Hectica solidis partibus & sit, vel Marasmus. Mercurio
translation: Hectic in solid parts & let it be, or Marasmus. By Mercury.

author: Claude Dariot
title: General Fever Treatment Rule
text: In curatione igitur ipsarum febrium, animadvertendum est diligenter, ne planeta morbum regens ac moderans, fortis in caelo sit: sed potius debilis, & planeta morbo repugnans & adversarius fortis ac fortunatus sit.
translation: In the cure, therefore, of these fevers, it must be diligently observed that the planet ruling and moderating the disease is not strong in the sky: but rather weak, and the planet opposing and adverse to the disease is strong and fortunate.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Quartan Fever Treatment Election
text: Veluti in principio curationis quartanae febris. Lunae ad Saturnum applicationes, & Saturni robur timendae sunt. Saturnus item neque ascendentis dominus sit, nec in eo vel caeli medio collocatus. Verumtamen Iupiter qui illi (ut antea dictum est) adversatur, roborandus est ac fortunandus in ascendente vel caeli medio, hoc est in decima domo. Et si ascendentem regere posset, praestantior operatio fieret. Quod si id fieri, non potest, ascendentem ac eius dominum respiciat. Eum etiam trino aut sextili aspectu Luna aspiciat, aut his aspectibus ei applicet.
translation: As in the beginning of the cure of quartan fever. The applications of the Moon to Saturn, and the strength of Saturn are to be feared. Likewise, Saturn should neither be the lord of the ascendant, nor placed in it or in the midheaven. However, Jupiter, who (as said before) is adverse to it, must be strengthened and made fortunate in the ascendant or midheaven, that is, in the tenth house. And if he could rule the ascendant, a more excellent operation would be performed. But if this cannot be done, let him regard the ascendant and its lord. Let the Moon also aspect him with a trine or sextile aspect, or apply to him with these aspects.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Moon's Condition for Fever Cure
text: A malis denique & infortuniis Luna sit libera, & in sui incremento lumine & numero aucta. Hac igitur via in reliquarum febrium curatione pergere oportet.
translation: Finally, let the Moon be free from evils and misfortunes, and increased in light and number in its increment. Therefore, one must proceed in this way in the cure of the remaining fevers.

author: Claude Dariot
title: Tertian and Quotidian Fever Treatment
text: Vt in febris tertianae curatione, Venus & Luna roborandae sunt, ac fortunandae. quo magis autem Mars erit debilior, eo magis prosperior curatio erit. In quotidiana vero curatione ordine contrario est procedendum. Luna tamen semper fortunata esse debet. Haec si quis diligenter animadverterit, non nisi prospere ac feliciter (favente Deo optimo & maximo) curatio succedet.
translation: As in the cure of tertian fever, Venus & Moon must be strengthened and made fortunate. The weaker Mars is, the more prosperous the cure will be. In the cure of quotidian fever, however, one must proceed in the contrary order. The Moon, however, must always be fortunate. If anyone diligently observes these things, the cure will succeed only prosperously and happily (with the favor of God, the best and greatest).
